topicQuestion: Does pineapple belong on pizza?

arguments.text: pineapple should not be on pizza because sweet toppings generally should not be on savoury food and warm pineapples are also disgusting
although i agree warm pineapples are an accquired taste, i think having a sweet element in a savoury food can complement it and enhance the whole flavour

i agree that warm pineapples are an acquired taste too. however, chances of having such a sharply contrasting food taste good is a large gamble. additionally, only 1 pizza has pineapples on it - hawaiian. true italian pizzerias would not have it in their stores.

hawaiian may be the only pizza with pineapple on it but it is somewhat popular. many pizzerias are now diversifying their flavours away from traditional italian ones. having pineapple can also cut the greasiness of pizza too

it is somewhat popular because the most convenient pizzas to obtain from i.e. fast food chains (think fast food pizzerias like dominos) and frozen pizzas. convenience and accessibility = more people consuming them = more people accepting this due to the conditioning of fast food chains and frozen food corporations.
pineapples being popular is a result of how it can complement pizza well. i think pineapple still belongs on pizza as it enhances the eating experience as it provides a juicy texture in the meats
the juicy texture from the meat comes from the meat itself. more often than not you taste the pineapple and then it covers up the taste of everything else due to how sharp it is.
i think the taste can be balanced if the pineapple is added sparingly so not every bite has it. i think without it, pizza can be too monotonous

reflections.paraphrase: sweet and savoury food should not mix
Consuming warm pineapples is a unique and personal choice. an addition of a contrasting flavour could also make the pizza taste better overall
having pineapple isnt traditional to its heritage
the one pizza that has pineapple on it is a crowd favourite which lends to the point that pineapples should be on pizza and more pizzerias are adopting this flavour too.
pineapple on pizza is only popular because of convenience
pineapples go well with pizza hence better eating experience
pineapple can be overwhelming the meaty flavours
pineapple adds dimension to the flavour profile

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

arguments.text: We should not use ChatGPT for homework because it has been proven that it has led to greater reliance on the AI at the expense of critical thinking skills of students. Students might outsource their thinking instead of having to learn and process information for themselves that is usually necessary in order to complete homework. This is foundational as a learning step and almost all recognized teaching pedagogies acknowledge the importance of thinking for oneself and thinking through problem

But in the age of AI, embracing AI and preparing students for the working world with AI is just as important. The critical aspect about using AI is the prompt and generating answers through "intelligent" prompt will help the students through their critical thinking process. How to craft out appropriate prompts are important. It’s like a critical part od learning too! To ask intelligent questions.

Sure, but not all students might be aware or even know how to intelligently use such prompts. While using AI intelligently is important, exposing students to use it for homework might be detrimental to them as they might end up being lazy instead of finding ways to use ChatGPT to complement their learning. This might be especially true for younger students as well.

Sure, maturity is certainly important. That’s why I believe our educational system should also change, the way homework is structured should also allow the complementary use of AI to improve learning. I certainly agree that it may be age appropriate.

Relying on an institutional change and hoping that our educational system will help mitigate such factors might be a bit of a stretch as well, seeing as kids have not been able to discern and use ChatGPT in a wholly beneficial manner. It seems best in their interest to not allow them access to such technology, specifically in the arena of homework where they might have to digest and learn things for themselves.

Influence on a country’s DNA stems from having strong institutions working their way through the system. Take for example, why we can exist as a multi-cultural society starts with NS, social cohesion program, racial quota, etc. So similarly, to prepare students for the real world with the inaugural new era of AI would have to start with educating them. What I’m advocating is embracing AI into student’s way of learning and homework will have to change from content heavy to thinking with AI.

While i acknowledge that having a strong institution would be able to adapt such shocks and help prepare students for the real world and deal with AI, there is still a time and place to introduce such things to them. While ChatGPT can indeed help with deep research and homework help, it still remains detrimental especially to younger students who may not have the know how and are exposed to its worst effects, leading to over reliance or even generating nonsense.
Yes, perhaps we need to move quickly to adapt this technology into our education system. Looking at ways to slowly ease in from an early age all the way to college.

reflections.paraphrase: As AI becomes more embedded in the world around us, preparing students for the working world is just as important. Using AI intelligently, in the form of "prompts" will help with the homework
We should not use as it will hamper our learning and critical thinking skills.
Using AI for homework is detrimental to learning resulting in the inability to learn.
reflections.acknowledgement: Somewhat agree to a certain extent.

reflections.paraphrase: Our educational system should be reformed in such a way that allows for greater flexibility and creativity in using AI for learning. This has to be tailored in such a way that makes it beneficial for the learner
Our students are still not mature enough to adopt AI in their homework. As a result it will disrupt their learning process.

Reforms done by an educational system stems from having it as a strong institution in the first place. It is necessary to start educating students about AI and allow them to use it such that they think with it instead of just throwing content at it.
We need to adapt ChatGPT into our educational system early and in a way that eases students and exposes them till college
For younger students they may not be able to adopt as yet.
reflections.acknowledgement: Somewhat. I agree on the younger students and also to less mature students even at higher learning.

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

arguments.text: We should use our own brains and exercise our own thought process.
But what if the homework requires me to find out more about a topic that i’ve engaged in before
We shouldn’t use ChatGPT because GAI models use a lot of natural resources that could be better used elsewhere.

AI is capable of offering me ideas that I myself might not have thought of before
Instead of using GAI models like ChatGPT, we could just use normal search engines like Google
It’s too troublesome to Google search what I’m looking for since AI models can just consolidate all the information for me

The homework requires her to use ChatGPT as a search tool to find more information that she has no prior knowledge about
Using AI models are detrimental to our environment
reflections.acknowledgement: was already aware of this point

reflections.paraphrase: AI helps to make her ideas better or more concise, and targeted to the requirements of the homework.
AI can generate novel ideas that we may not have heard about previously
Using AI too often makes us reliant on it
Google is a more sustainable alternative to using AI
Using normal search engines is more tedious. ChatGPT can summarise all its findings with just one prompt

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

arguments.text: Chatgpt is like a modern dictionary or calculators. If calculators are allowed for mathematics homework, then why not chatgpt?
Ok fair calculator is an aid for student, but one may argue that students may become too reliant on chatgpt and not use their critical thinking to solve their homework and not learn anything
You can code the LLM which is the technology that chatgpt is based on to not spoon feed the answer to the students.
But why would the students do that or what if the students can find a workaround like use their parents chatgpt? Then they will just rely on it fully.

Then it is the parents fault, and it is no different from parents helping them do the homework at this point. The kid should just use the dedicated and special chatgpt which can be coded to foster critical thinking

Ok fair enough, going back to you calculator example, in primary school we cannot use calculator - so clearly when it comes to some cases these instruments should not be used because it limits the childs learning. So maybe some usage is good but not complete reliance.

That’s fair, I think we can agree to limit the usage of chatgpt at higher education levels. Chatgpt maybe shouldn’t be used for homework but it should still be used to help students understand and learn.
Yeah chatgpt usage must be moderated by adults aka parents and teachers to facilitate learning

reflections.paraphrase: Chatgpt can be a good tool for students so why not
Chatgpt is a double edged sword?
You can curb full reliance of chatgpt when students use it
The student can bypass the restriction by using unrestricted LLMs
Education tools are only used at higher levels. Hence chatgpt shouldn’t be allowed for lower education
Aid is also given via parents, so no difference when using chatgpt. And chatgpt usage can be limited and up to parents to enforce
Ok
Limit chatgpt usage to higher education

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?

arguments.text: A higher pay enables a higher income. A higher income is an enabler for many factors in a high quality of living. These include providing good healthcare for your family, being able to provide for children’s education needs, freedom to travel to places you want to and live a lifestyle that you want. Further, less social life doesn’t mean no social life. It is very possible to have an enriching social life with a tighter circle of friends, even with less time overall.

I can think of families where your argument would make sense, especially where there that higher income could help meet some basic needs. However, I think there are many intangible (and priceless) goods in life, that can’t be bought, like the joy of spending time with family and friends, artistic and creative pursuits, and serving others, that make up a full and flourishing life. If taking a higher pay means having less time, this might mean less time available for these other important areas.

Certainly, many things cannot be bought, but the option space increases significantly for someone with a higher income. Imagine a 10% cut in time but 40% growth in options. While the time spent with family inherently is the good, it is greatly supported by context of the time spent. Think back to your best memories with your family, many would probably recall holidays or activities, all of which a higher income can help. Also, many creative pursuits require income.

I agree that there are some situations where a higher income would enable a higher quality of life overall, even at some expense to one’s social life. To move this forward — what do you think are the kinds of situations where making this tradeoff is worthwhile? For example, perhaps the duration of earning more with less time could be a factor, and one’s other responsibilities. I wonder if even just the experience of working an earning more could be formative (and not in a good way).

This eventually boils down to quite an individual decision. However, I would say that some sort of measure of "diffusion" of social life could be used. If one finds themselves drained by certain social interactions or having very superficial connections/obligations, they should consider whether it is actually worthwhile to pare down on their social activities, in exchange for a higher-paying job. Finding a point where the benefits of their social life begins to saturate may make sense.

That’s a great heuristic, and the scenario you describe is one where (to me) it would be worth considering being more involved at work. At the same time, I wonder if an alternative response to that scenario might not be to ’pare down’ relationships through the matrix of "what’s good for me", but to explore other ways to get engaged. Often, accepting more responsibilities at work — even with a higher pay — is a decision that might be hard to reverse; for one, because of lifestyle inflation.

True, I have taken a rational, personal utility-maximizing argument so far. However, I think no matter what new ways you find to engage, both social life and an elevated lifestyle eventually saturate or reach diminishing marginal returns. And this is true for yourself and your loved ones. True that the decision may be hard to reverse, I’ll give that. However I would say that the alternative of more time does not guarantee a more fulfilling social life if your loved ones are not on the same page.

I think that, while one can burn out at work, one can always fill one’s remaining time with a combination of personal challenges, hobbies, strengthening meaningful relationships, and otherwise. It’s true that one can definitely swing too far to one side, and be under-employed to the point of not providing for one’s family and other personal responsibilities.

reflections.paraphrase: Tangible, priceless goods that can’t be bought.

One: a higher income can purchase many goods that improve one’s (and one’s family’s) standard of living.
Two: how much ’social life’ is needed is subjective — one can earn more and still have a social life.
It is possible to give up some time to earn more, in some situations.
Questions the context to which this would be a worthwhile decision.
Lifestyle inflation is a good point. Locked in

My opponent advocates a framework of deciding if one’s social life is already saturated (e.g. an introvert with too many interactions, or many superficial ones) in deciding if one can reasonably take on more work (and more pay).
reflections.acknowledgement: Yes, some of that seemed reasonable to me.

reflections.paraphrase: My opponent argued that you can go too far in either direction.
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, I agree with them!

reflections.paraphrase: Striking a balance between prioritising work and outside pursuits

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

arguments.text: Yes, we should use ChatGPT for homework, at least in college. GenAI is the potentially the most consequential productivity multiplier we have seen in the third industrial revolution, and it will impact every field. It is imperative that students begin to adopt genAI in their workflows now, so they are prepared to use it in the workplace.

I agree that generative AI is an essential skill for the future and is worth learning, but I don’t think the students should use it in homework because of that. I think the purpose of (some types) of homework is to develop students’ ability to think, and using ChatGPT (to devise an algorithm, to write a paper) would short-circuit that process — just like handing children a calculator would do the same for learning arithmetic.

It is true that full dependence on ChatGPT could potentially short-circuit students ability to think. At the same time there is a counterbalancing force of enabling accessibility to a broader search space of ideas and rapid discovery. We ought to train students how to use these tools effectively and not mindlessly. Just like kids eventually pick up a calculator, students, especially college students should be made to learn effective, thoughtful ChatGPT use.

Definitely, ChatGPT can let students access varied ideas, and more rapidly. And I agree they should be given the freedom to eventually experiment how to use them. But I think using ChatGPT (and other technologies) could not only short-circuit their learning to think, but also social and relational skills — as children who would have otherwise consulted teachers and peers for help now just ask an LLM. I think the risks outweigh the benefits, since it happens at a developmentally crucial stage.

That is a fair point. Talking to ChatGPT certainly does not build social and relational skills. But again, this should not preclude its usage entirely. There is also the possibility of human-machine collaboration/co-evolution, which could involve not just man interfacing with machine, but groups of people collaborating with AI. This is a facet that could develop both the ways we relate with one another as well as machines at the same time.

I agree that human-machine collaboration is an essential for anyone entering the workforce today. I think that even then, there are benefits to limiting AI use for a time to learn effective collaboration between humans, before learning to collaborate with machines (instead of trying to do both).

I think one larger question that lies behind what we’ve discussed so far is, What is education for? I think that beyond preparing students for work, it has a large developmental and formative role too.

If we are talking about collaboration skills for the workforce, we are (maybe unfortunately) in a time where the bulk of collaboration is not AI-free. So the bulk of training should be preparing for such a dynamic. It is true that education does have a role beyond preparation for work. But I don’t think this comes from the homeworks themselves. A lot of it may be through informal conversations, participation in student life activities, working in groups (which doesn’t go away).

I think it’s fair to say most collaboration will involve AI of some kind, and that it’s worth training for. Ijust think the place to do it is once people have entered the workforce, where they will be able to pick them up just as quickly.

And, even if students are disadvantaged in the workforce for lack of practice: I think it’s worthwhile, since using AI in homework irreversibly changes the tenor of many social interactions that happen only in school, e.g. asking for their friends’ help less.

reflections.paraphrase: They think that chatgpt would short circuit the ability of students to think.
Generative AI is a essential skill to work well in the new economy, and students need practice to use it well.
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, because I agree with his point!

reflections.paraphrase: Students ought to be trained to use tools effectively, and not mindlessly.
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, because I still think the risks outweigh the benefits.

reflections.paraphrase: Social and relational skills maybe superseded by LLM.
They think there are benefits to limiting AI use to learn effective collaboration between humans.
Learning to collaborate with machines (not just outsourcing thought to AI) is an essential skill, and is worth practicing to be done well.
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, because I think there’s a larger purpose to education beyond just learning to do jobs well.

reflections.paraphrase: Some things are irreversible
Most future collaboration will involve some kind of AI, so it’s worth training for that in school, too.
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, because I think even if students are disadvantaged in the workforce, they retain something of more value.

topicQuestion: Does pineapple belong on pizza?

postDebateSurvey.player1DetectionOther: this debate was done by known opponent
postDebateSurvey.player2DetectionOther: This debate was done with a known opponent

arguments.text: it gives texture and sweetness to the pizza. the pineapple actually complements very well with the other ingredients.
Other savoury foods like mushroom can give a similar texture without having such a different flavour profile.

having pineapples on pizza is more than providing texture. it helps to cut the cut the grease of the oilness of the other ingredients and provides a refreshing boost to the flavour profile of the pizza, making it more enjoyable.

If you want a refreshing burst of flavours and less cloying greasiness, the tangy margherita sauce that many tomato-based pastas have already does a good job. A sweet item like pineapple only makes the entire flavour profile of the pizza more confusing.

the margherita sauce is more savoury than sweet and so it should not replace the pineapples but instead be used together with the pineapples. tomatoes don’t cut through the fat and salt of cheese in the pizza as well as pineapples because they are less sharp and distinct in flavour.

Margherita sauce is indeed more savoury than sweet, but perhaps we do not need a sweet flavour profile to begin with -- tanginess/savouriness is enough. The combination of tomatoes and cheese is also quite well-known; we see it in dishes like bolognese with parmesan, or in a caprese salad. In those dishes, we don’t need added sweetness from anything so likewise we should not need it for pizzas.

pizza is pizza. bolognese is bolognese. they are 2 different dishes. what makes pizza unique is the different ingredients that can be added to it, and the different flavour profiles that we can achieve. i think the combination of adding pineapples to pizza is really unique as it not only provides a good balance to the savouriness of the pizza but also helps to cut the grease of fat and salt of cheese in the pizza -- this is something no other ingredient is able to replicate.

I agree that the beauty of pizzas is that multiple ingredients can be added to it. However, harmony of flavour is important, and if pineapples add a sweet-sour taste to the already-present savoury and tangy profiles, the result could be too overwhelming/confusing for the eater. Focused flavours are important and overly-complex flavour combinations could actually be less enjoyable.

reflections.paraphrase: Pineapple adds texture and sweetness. It also goes well with other ingredients.
reflections.acknowledgement: Slightly, as it I could not really deny the point on pineapple complementing the flavour of the other ingredients.

reflections.paraphrase: there are many other different ingredient options apart from pineapples that can add a different dimension to the pizza
Pineapples do more than provide texture; they are refreshing and help reduce the grease of the pizza.
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, as I felt that there were good substitutes (like margherita sauce) for the same function that was mentioned.

reflections.paraphrase: adding pineapples will do no good to the flavour profile of the pizza. the margherita sauce is also refreshing.
if dishes like bolognese with parmesan don’t require anything sweet, then there is no need for pizzas to contain anything sweet like pineapples

Margherita sauce and pineapples serve two different purposes (one is savoury and one is sweet) and hence their effects are not repetitive. Pineapples are better at cutting through salt and fat as they are sharper and more distinct in flavour.
reflections.acknowledgement: I could not really refute either point, so I could only present a separate argument.

reflections.paraphrase: pineapples disrupt the tastebuds of those who are looking for something savoury.
The uniqueness of pizzas is their customisability and combination of different flavours, and pineapples contribute to that.
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really; although it is a true statement I did not feel that pineapples were necessarily an improvement to the overall flavour profile of the pizza

Time is of the essence. Money can’t buy everything.

topicQuestion: Does pineapple belong on pizza?

arguments.text: In my personal opinion, pineapple on pizza distorts the original taste of the dough and the other ingredients. It can cheapen the other ingredients such as pastrami, especially for pizzas that occupy a higher price point with more premium ingredients.

So your definition of pineapple belonging on pizza means that wherever pizza is served, they should also put pineapple on it. I am quite loose on whether it is a universal application though. I definitely disagree with that premise. I imagine sitting in Pizza Hut or ordering delivery and among the pepperoni and margherita is hawaiian. That’s the best way to enjoy it.

I can definitely see your point though and I agree that there isn’t a universal application. To shift the parameters, foods are considered to be fusion foods when traditional modes of preparation and traditional ingredients are adapted to include that from other cultures. So what then can we can traditionally consider to be pizza? Is the Hawaiian Pizza a pizza or a form of fusion cuisine?
That’s a good point on fusion food. I would make the distinction that Hawaiian pizza wouldn’t be a fusion of Italian pizza and Hawaiian food, but rather a cultural interpretation of pizza.

I can definitely agree with that because, after all, food is not something that we can define with strict parameters. So in taking the lens that a Hawaiian Pizza is a cultural interpretation of a pizza, I find it prudent to ascribe the loose definition of the traditional pizza to that we can find in Italy. If a large majority of Italians feel that the pineapple has no place on the pizza, how much can we deviate from that view before the food item is no longer a pizza?

Ahh. So in that sense, pizza would be a flat piece of bread that is baked in an oven with either red or white sauce and toppings on top. In this definition, it is justifiable to use whatever toppings you prefer. Kind of like Subway. Have it your way!

Yes, I could definitely agree with these parameters. I think personally, taking the Italian view, pizza is better without pineapple. 

I can see a lot of value in your point because taste is a very subjective measure. So while I do not prefer pineapple on pizza, I do not think its necessarily a bad thing - just a subjective measure that I may not prescribe to.
Okay so we can decide that whether a topping belongs on pizza is a matter of taste. So while it doesn’t suit your taste, it’s not wrong to say pineapple belongs on pizza for me.

reflections.paraphrase: Changes the taste of pizza and it may cheapen the premium ingredients
My opponent argues that there is no universal application for what constitutes a food. I agree but have some reservations which I will address af a later point.
Is pineapple on pizza a fusion food?
My opponent tried to broaden the parameters of what we consider a pizza
reflections.acknowledgement: Not so much but he raise good points

reflections.paraphrase: If italy is the home of the pizza, how many Italians would it take to change the definition of pizza to include pineapples on it?
The definition of what constitutes pizza doesnt matter so much - it is subjective.
reflections.acknowledgement: Yes. I think I could see commonalities in the points raised.

reflections.paraphrase: Taste is subjective
We came to a strong agreement that the topic is subjective.

topicQuestion: Does pineapple belong on pizza?

arguments.text: i think pineapples belong on pizza because like the pizza already so unhealthy. you need to accumulate some health points help your digestive system pass the junk food. Anyways, pineapples tastes good on anything so adding it on pizza makes pizza even better!

i do agree that pizzas are unhealthy food but i don’t think adding a few slices of pineapple on it will make more than a marginal difference in its health content. if you wanted to eat something healthy, you’re better off not eating pizza altogether. and i disagree that pineapple tastes good on anything, it’s a subjective opinion. in my opinion, pineapple doesn’t taste good on pizzas so.

I agree that if i wanted something healthier, I would go for something other than pizza (e.g. a salad). However, pizza is almost always a group affair. So like if my friend group decides to eat pizza then I can’t be the stick in the mud and go order something else. And given i’m going to have to have pizza anyways, why not have some healthy pineapple on top

if you want a "healthier pizza" option, you could go for pizza toppings that incorporate more vegetables rather than just pineapples. the notion of pizzas being a sharing dish is also not true everywhere, ie. in italy, it is the norm for one individual to have a pizza all to themself and it is frowned upon for tourists to order one pizza for sharing

I agree that there is always an option to include more vegetables. I also agree that in some places, people don’t share pizza. However, in a Singapore context, I feel like most occasions where pizza is served, pizza is shared. Additionally the pineapple crunch and tanginess is a much welcomed flavour contrast with the savoury/salty pizza.

ok i accept the parameters that singapore is the context of our debate. and pizza is indeed shared here more often than not. i don’t accept however the argument that pineapples contrast well with the savouriness of pizzas. subjectively, i prefer consistency in my food’s flavour profiles and the contrast pineapples offer with their sweetness is too great a contrast. food does well to have some contrast but not too much

I agree that that too much contrast is not good. However, there isn’t that much pineapple included anyways. If you should dislike having them on your pizza, you can take easily them out or put them on another slice. But for someone who prefers pineapple on pizza, it’s easier for the pineapple to be already there than to have a separate pineapple ready every time it’s time for pizza.

if i could just take out the pinapples from my pizza, i would be wasting food. and i could just opt to not have any in the first place. i think it’s not actually a fairer decision to order pineapple toppings just for the person who prefers because then the person who doesn’t prefer has to adjust. while it may be easier to already have the pineapples there, it’s not entirely reasonable or food-conscious to expect others to adjust to your tastes. either compromise or do without the unwanted extra.

reflections.paraphrase: My anonymous opponent thinks that if one wants something healthy, he/she should buy something else other than pizza. Additionally, he/she does not think that pineapples taste good on anything.
my opponent believes that adding pineapples to pizzas might elevate the dish in terms of both health content and taste.
reflections.acknowledgement: no, the increase in health content is negligible, and the taste argument is subjective

reflections.paraphrase: pineapples on pizzas are the "healthier choice" if getting pizza is the only option you have, eg in a group setting. if they’re having pizza anyways, best to get pineapples with it for health reasons
reflections.acknowledgement: no, there are still healthier options than pineapples

reflections.paraphrase: My opponent posited that there’s always a healthier vegetable pizza option. And in some places, pizza is not shared, so one gets to choose what kind of pizza he/she wants
singaporean pizza norms are such that pizzas are shared, and pineapples contrast well with pizzas in terms of flavour
my opponent agrees that pizza is often shared in the singapore context. However, he/she prefers to have pizza with a non-contrastive flavour profile
not much pineapple added to induce too great a flavour contrast and it’s easier to remove pineapples than it is to add them after the fact
Taking out the pineapples is wasting food. It will be better to just order pineapples for the person who wants them on their pizza.

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?

arguments.text: I believe that as humans, what we’re searching for is happiness. If we do have higher pay, there isn’t any point if we’re not happy. If we have a very high pay but no time to spend, how will we enjoy life? That defeats the entire purpose of higher pay!

My counter-agrument is applicable for the majority of singaporeans who are a lower-middle incomed SES group who have presssing costs that need to be met before considering how they can spend their time with family and friends pursuing happiness. Higher pay is the gateway to ensure our basic needs are met
i disagree, what would it profit me to gain the world but lose my soul
how do you define profit? when we interpret scripture, we cannot apply it to any context that we want. They were specifically talking about their relationship with the God of Jesus Christ.
Imagine earning millions of dollars but not being able to enjoy it. VS Earning few thousands, but being able to enjoy life. You do not need riches to enjoy life.
how can you enjoy life if you do not have the funds to support your medical treatment that grants you many more days to spend with your family
buy insurance

where does the constant income to fund insurance comes from?
even MHA is trying to put a curb to high insurance premiums:
"To address rising insurance premiums and private healthcare costs, the Ministry of Health (MOH) is putting a stop to health insurance plans that protect policyholders almost “to the last dollar”.

reflections.paraphrase: I agree that pay is important. But it is also important to manage our costs and expectations. Yes, with higher pay comes freedom, but it does not guarantee happiness. While low-middle incomed people do not have higher pay and may struggle to make ends meets, if they are still able to enjoy life, then it is still good. Lower pay does not necessarily equal to being poorer. And if you are in a position of being in need, then higher pay would make more sense, and you should focus your time to earn money so you could live to see tomorrow. But if you are already earning enough, then it’s time to enjoy life, there is no point in pursuing something that is never ending.

Its true that higher pay will not always grant you happiness from the opponents point. His concern is the time spent making money that takes away the time from our families. At the end of the day i believe everyone has the choice to either make time, or choose jobs that can match the expectations of spending enough time at work and with the family
reflections.acknowledgement: At the end of the day, theres nothing inherently wrong with pursuing higher pay, as long as it isnt self-centered and at the expense of others, everyone has the choice on how they make time to pursuse happiness outside of work

reflections.paraphrase: Question states: Higher pay at the expense of social life. If you’re gaining a ridiculous amount of money but have no time for relationships with friends/church/God, what is the point? There isn’t any point in gaining so much money but not being able to use it to ENJOY life. Enjoy the life that God has given you!
i agree with his point that the ultimate goal of our soul should not be corrupted by anything else but God’s will for our life.
you cannot enjoy life that way but isn’t the point of life to enjoy it? id rather live a short enjoyable life than live a long life of misery
cmon man insurance aint that expensive tho

topicQuestion: Does pineapple belong on pizza?

arguments.text: The Italians think pineapple on pizza is an abomination and an affront to their Italian heritage. Since Italians invented the pizza, the rest of us should respect their preferences, otherwise who are we to impose our own "blending" on their culture?

I believe that food 🥑 is not just for the Italians, but for everyone. It should not be the case that the Italians can insist that pineapple must not be put on pizza, in spite of or because of the fact that they invented it or that pizza is part of Italian culture. Non-Italians are free to make their own food as they please, that pineapple or any other food topping can be put on pizza if they wish

I agree that food is meant for everyone to enjoy, not just one culture or people group. But I believe that the Italians probably had a good reason for not wanting a tropical fruit on their pizza. they would have cooked it up originally with a specific taste in mind, hence their confusion over why pineapple is so beloved on pizza. we should trust that they have a good reason, especially if we want to enjoy it the way the "inventors" intended.

Maybe the Italians had their reason to not put pineapple on pizza. But that does not mean that other people think it is wrong to put pineapple on pizza; in fact many people love Hawaiian pizza for it’s tangy pineapple together with tender ham. If many other people think that pineapple on pizza tastes good while Italians and others think it’s bad, the question of whether pineapple belongs on pizza may not have a definite answer that applies to everyone; one’s own opinion can be correct either way

I agree with you that we can all have our own opinions and it might not apply to everyone. But if I told you spaghetti sauce belongs on rice, how would you respond? 

I think pineapple doesn’t belong on pizza because it’s tanginess overwhelms the taste of all other ingredients including the cheese. The worth of a pizza is in its cheese, therefore all toppings should do nothing more than complement the main flavor, not overwhelmed it.

I think that cheese shouldn’t be the only thing that stands out in pizza with all other toppings enhancing the cheese flavour - then what would be the point in having multiple types of pizzas if they all mainly taste of cheese? Maybe pizzas can also showcase other toppings like mushrooms, or yes, pineapple, with cheese remaining in the background.  Surely there are other toppings that can enhance the tangy flavour of pineapple or blend well with it in pizza

I do like having variety in my flavours. Based on your previous argument I think I would agree with you. One of the best things about pizza is how much variety there is in the different flavours and combinations of toppings. But then, is ham really the best topping to balance out pineapple? What about beef, or bacon, or even seafood?

I haven’t thought about other meat toppings other than ham, maybe bacon or thin fried strips of beef/chicken could give a crispy taste that pairs well with tangy pineapple. Whatever the other toppings I think we can agree that pineapple can belong on pizza to shine as the main taste

reflections.paraphrase: since food is not culturally or nationally exclusive, Italians’ tastes should not prevent other cultures from enjoying the dish the way they want to
Pizza was invented by Italians is important to Italian culture, so Italians should get to insist that pineapple does not belong on pizza as they don’t like pineapple on pizza
reflections.acknowledgement: Nothing changed for me, my opponent’s comments strengthened my opinion

reflections.paraphrase: Italians though pineapple on pizza would taste bad, so they did not put pineapple on pizza initially, and we should take the Italians’ view as the inventors of pizza that pineapple does not belong on pizza
reflections.acknowledgement: Slightly, I see that some people think it’s wrong but others think it correct

reflections.paraphrase: We can all have our own preferences. we are not bound by what someone says. An opinion doesn’t mean it’s correct, therefore he’s not wrong to say that pineapple can be on pizza
Tanginess of pineapple overwhelms the other ingredients and cheese, when cheese should be the main flavour
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, there are other pizzas which taste mostly of truffle, with cheese in the background

reflections.paraphrase: Pineapple adds to the variety of tastes of pizza. Pizza is not supposed to be restricted to just cheese flavor.
Opponent agrees that pineapple/other toppings can give variety to pizza, but thinks that ham may not be the best to pair with pineapple
reflections.acknowledgement: No, opponent agreed with my view

reflections.paraphrase: alternatives to ham are possible to match with pineapple but pineapple should be the main taste

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

arguments.text: chatgpt makes for a great study buddy to bounce ideas off of. I’m not for cutting corners, but for facilitating critical thinking.

I actually agree that it makes a good study buddy, but the question is how many of us use it purely as a study buddy? Humans often go for the path of least resistance. If ChatGPT can help them with the thinking portions of assignments, why wouldn’t they use it for that? It is difficult for anyone to draw a clean boundary between discussion and delegation.

Using AI helps speed through the process of learning, so more topics and subjects can be explored in the same amount of time. In the current age of information and big data, education should focus on filtering data on what’s useful. Depth of knowledge will soon be delegated to AI.

Again, I largely agree with what you said. However, where is the distinction between quick exploration for organic learning as opposed to quick generation for getting a grade? If we want to best use AI for learning, we have to fundamentally overhaul education to enable students to use AI as an augmentation for their cognition versus a crutch.

Since AI is here to stay, why don’t people learn to use it? I think it is wrong to rely on it to generate homework answers because it doesn’t engage critical thinking, but there is some value in getting used to having it around. Many university graduates who were banned from using AI find it hard to adapt in the workplace that heavily relies on AI for day-to-day tasks.

This is true. AI should be integrated into homework if and only if we overhaul what homework looks like. We need to design assignments that still challenge and train and hone students’ critical thinking abilities. But yes, giving them fluency in AI tools could definitely be useful for the economy that we are living in now.

Great! So we agree that AI usage is useful for students as it is a useful tool. This allows for more interdisciplinary exploration, where previously STEM students can explore creative fields like visual art or cinema based on ideas they have, but no chance to realise them. Similarly, students in creative fields can have technical concepts broken down into terms that are personalised to them. This greatly enhances learning across the board wouldn’t you agree?

Do note the nuance. I say AI can be used for homework only if and only if we overhaul what current homework looks like. If homework stays the same, then no we should not be using AI in significant ways for homework because it encourages dependence. Interdisciplinary exploration can be valuable, but we also risk diluting each of these fields with mass produced content without craft. Should students really be able to so-called realise all of their ideas without understanding the value of process?
Well yes. I’m very sure that the creative process is valuable, if not more than the result. What I am saying, however, is that AI usage reduces the accessibility to weaker fields of study.

I think the most value in terms of interdisciplinary learning comes in the form of collaboration with other people from other disciplines. If students merely collaborate with AI to cross the disciplinary bounds, then we are losing the chance for interpersonal connection and synergy.
But it does spark interest, wouldn’t it? Hopefully students don’t stop at just using AI. I’m satisfied with this compromise.

reflections.paraphrase: hard to enforce chatgpt’s identity as a study buddy rather than asking it for answers
They say that ChatGPT is a good study buddy and facilitates critical thinking.
They think that AI speeds the process of learning, which i agree with.
hard to differentiate between exploring topics and cheating at homework
My opponent said that we should be able to get used to using AI. And it helps us to adapt.
agrees with me that incorporating AI in homework will be upskilling for uni students. requires homework to adapt to era of AI
reflections.acknowledgement: it is my view so yes

reflections.paraphrase: pushing back on my point by stating a previously established assumption
They say that it allows interdisciplinary learning.
They say that the creative process makes more accessible?
no human connection in AI usage

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?

arguments.text: A higher pay enables more choices in life. We are able to choose the best path for our families, and to invest in things we care about. Also, it makes sense to invest time in a smaller group of close friends instead of spreading ourselves thin worrying about the lives of people we will never really be that close to.
Being highly connected socially has proven to be an indicator of better standard of living. Material wealth is not a holistic measure of happiness and quality of life.

What I’m hearing is that social connection is similarly a factor in better standard of living, but also not a holistic measure. It is true that material wealth is not a holistic measure, but it is an important factor of happiness and quality of life. A rich person will not automatically be happy, but all things held equal someone with a higher income is likely to be able to make more choices that make them happier. Also, why does a higher income necessarily come at the expense of social life?

I think you are backtracking in your argument. Your previous claim was to minimise your social circle and to aim for higher pay. If I’m not mistaken, you are now saying that it isn’t a matter of choosing one over the other, but striking a balance. Where do you find a balance between the two?

Unlike other factors that contribute to better standard of living, a higher income provides two levels of benefits. 1) the higher income in itself grants individuals a sense of self-worth and agency, a validation of their competencies and value they bring to the table. 2) Wealth is an instrumental goal which enables the pursuit of other goals that contribute to quality of life. For example, if an individual enjoys travelling with family, wealth enables them to pursue that goal.

I find it troubling that higher income grants one a "sense of self-worth and agency". My influence is more Aristotelian: there is inherent dignity in all. On that point we can disagree. Granted, money does open doors for travelling, the foundation of your argument is individualistic, ie your higher pay is for you to enjoy. Sure. But why spend your whole life on lifestyle inflation? It leaves one emptier for chasing what they can never grasp in their hands.

Higher income is an external signal to for one’s sense of self-worth and agency. A heavy dumbbell on the floor doesn’t make you strong, but being able to lift a 30kg dumbbell vs a 15kg one can help you see yourself getting stronger. A higher income provides a similar signal for your competencies and skill. Your argument seems to assume that wealth is only for selfish gain. The example being about providing better for family already moots that point.

One can provide for family with average and low pay. Higher pay implies a discontentment with life and follows our society’s capitalistic metanarrative that more money leads to happiness. With prudent financial management, one can lead a fuss free life without having to pay the opportunity cost of hustling more in career. AND spend time at home with the family and community too. On your point on competencies and skill, I disagree. There are many incompetent managers in the corporate world.

Let’s look at the claims one by one.
1) The idea that "One can provide for family with average and low pay" comes across as extraordinarily privileged. In which country or society can we actually assume that is true? And by provide do we mean the bare minimum?
2) We have already established that more money doesn’t by itself lead to more happiness (see round 3), but saying that someone only seeks a higher pay because they are discontent discounts some’s healthy desire for growth.

1. Okay let’s play ball. What do the happiest countries in the world value? Definitely not capitalism nor high pay. My contention is grounded in desiring to thrive in life; if money takes up too much of your goals, it creates unrest and anxiety and an inability to live in the present moment. Furthermore, if money really is the path to happiness, why aren’t more first-world countries happier?
2. Why grow your money? Where does it end? One million dollars? Two?

1) The first point is moot. For example, Singapore is pretty consistently ranked as one of the happiest nations in Asia. It is also one of the highest-income countries in a world. Pick from any of the Scandinavian countries and you will find they likely appear in the top 10-20th percentile for both happiness and income
2) As mentioned, money is an instrumental goal that enables you to do things that make you. You may have 10% less time but 50% more choices.
1. stress la bro. 2. some things can’t be bought with money. For example, time spent with elderly parents. No amount of money can bring them back.

2. That is certainly true, but there are also many things that cannot be obtained with time. For instance, making sure your parents’ medical needs are fully met with a good insurance plan. You may be content with buying them a basic policy until they end up in the hospital with a debilitating illness and rising medical bills. Seeing a high income as important is too often demonized and not seen as being a practical, significant factor in building quality of life.

reflections.paraphrase: Keep your circle small and earn high pay
The point they are making is that social connection is a proven indicator of standard of living, and that wealth is not complete.
higher pay means more happiness
The opponent thinks that my argument is about balance. I have to reframe my argument to make it stronger
reflections.acknowledgement: not really, more what direction to take the debate

reflections.paraphrase: higher pay gives people meaning in life and a means to achieve your goals
they think that income is individualistic pursuit that is ultimately empty.
higher income means you have high competencies and skills and desiring wealth isn’t individualistic if you use it to provide for your family
They say that it is possible to provide for family with average and low pay. They say that demanding a higher income is merely discontentment.
families can’t be provided for with low pay, and people who desire high pay desire growth
The opponent attacks the claim using poor counterexamples. The opponent tries to use a slippery slope fallacy to attack the claim.
high income and high happiness index; money increases your choices
They think that some things cannot be bought with money and thus money is not as important.

topicQuestion: Does pineapple belong on pizza?

arguments.text: We should respect the people who created pizza. Their recipe does not include pineapples. By adding pineapples we are disrespecting their creative and intellectual property. However I understand that pineapple may taste good on pizza for some.

while it is important to respect the people who created pizza, as the world evolves so must the food. That is not to say that we completely disregard traditional pizza. There will always be a place for that but we need to also embrace the modern world and food. We are simply creating another version of pizza. Its history and tradition is not lost but we are also keeping up with times and peoples taste preferences.

I agree that food must evolve as the world does and food is designed for preferences. However, if it is another version of pizza with a different recipe, calling it pizza is a misrepresentation. Pizza refers to a food made according the Italian recipie. By changing the recipie, you are no longer making pizza. Hence, by continuing to call it pizza, you disregard the original recipie.

There is no original recipe for pizza only for specific types of pizza like Neapolitan pizza. Those specific pizzas was what was eaten traditionally. Pizza just refers to dough topped with toppings. Also who is to say that the people who created pizza are against having pineapples on pizza? That idea back then could have maybe been so unique that they did not even consider it. Perhaps if they knew the balance it brings to the flavour they would have enjoyed it?

If the definition of pizza is dough with toppings, then a pork floss bun is pizza. But it isnt, so we see that pizza refers to a specific way of treating the dough, and specific kinds of toppings on the dough. Pizza is eaten at home with leftover dough and readily accessible ingredients such as olives, so pineapple which does not grow in Italy would have never been added. Hence there is an original recipe and pineapple would not have been a part.

I think I was a little too vague in my argument. Of course pizza refers to a specific way of treating the dough but I do not agree about specific kinds of toppings on the dough. Most of the food we eat nowadays is not necessarily cooked using the same ingredients or in the same way as they traditionally were but its name is still the same. What we are eating are just constant iterations of the original product.

It’s true that many traditional dishes have altered ingredients. However, the reason why pizza with pineapple is no longer pizza is that the taste has changed. If the taste of a dish remains largely similar then it can continue to be called that. Risotto cooked with asian ingredients is still risotto if it preserves the intended flavour. However, pineapple tastes strongly sweet and acidic, significantly altering the taste profile of the pizza. If no taste liek pizza, is not pizza.

In different parts of the world that original taste of pizza might not be so widely enjoyed. It would be sad to see the history of pizza lost simply over taste. If we have narrow view on what a pizza should be we risk losing it completely in the future. It is because of adding ingredients not commonly added traditionally that we are having this debate on pizza. If it means changing how pizza is made very slightly to suit different peoples taste so pizza will continued to be "remembered" so be it

reflections.paraphrase: The main point was that we need to respect tradition and the people who made the pizza
It is okay for food to change and tastes and preferences change. Culture and origins are not disrespected in that process.
Opponent stated that pizza has a fixed recipe and changing that recipe would not make it pizza and so it would not be accurate to have pineapples on pizza and call that a pizza

- The definition of Pizza is not restricted to traditional pizzas, rather it generically refers to dough with toppings.
- Speculatively italians may have included pineapples on pizzas, so why shld we exclude them just because they didnt find that idea then?
The opponent talked about making pizza the traditional way and using ingredients found in the origin country and so argued that there is an original recipe
A dish can change in its ingredients but keep its name.
Oponent takes a firm stand on how pizza is prepared and with what ingredients and doing it any other way would not make it a pizza. Especially if the taste is different
By creatively altering the recipe we can allow pizza to continue

topicQuestion: Does pineapple belong on pizza?

arguments.text: Pineapple does not belong on pizza because it was not originally on pizza.
We are aligned that we like pizza. I argue that there needs to be a sweet flavour to balance to savouriness of the cheese and other ingredients in a pizza.

I acknowledge you that we both like pizza. However, in this culture of relativism, there is a sense of deciding what is right for you and no one can speak against "your truth". I believe that relativism is the fall of society and there should be an institution that determines what things should be. My default would be the Italian culinary world. Deviations to this definition cannot be called pizza.
That being said, this means everyone has to agree  on what is the true institution. Which leads to a debate on who gives these people the right to decide what is true.
Okay, sounds good. I think it is justified that the Italians decide what pizza should be because it was started by them.

Why can’t Italian pizza and pineapple on pizza co-exist? Definitions evolve over time in different cultures. As pizza is a globalised phenomenon, different people use what’s available and what’s in their native palates to build a pizza that is right for them.
But what you are suggesting is a contradiction because you are trying to make a compromise between objectivity and subjectivity. This could result in chaos.
Thats not true. Both are definitely allowed to exist. Different cultures can have chilli on hotdogs or not, and that’s fine. Chicago and New York still stand. This isn’t the Wild West, this is food.

I see your point that chili exists on hot dogs. But you don’t eat hot dogs in restaurants. Hot dogs are universally known to be street food, whereas pizza is established as a culinary icon of Italy. This supports the sentiment I shared about accepting the definition of a pizza as a traditional Italian food.

Pizza is more than tradition now. There is pizza in all parts of the world! Why should we have to submit to Italy? It’s gone beyond them, and if it’s Italy vs the world, the world will win. Italy will have to accept that the world will progress. Maybe they can keep the traditions alive, but the rest of us will be enjoying whatever toppings we want on our pizzas.

reflections.paraphrase: wasnt on it in the first place
pineapple balances the flavour of pizza
only italians should decide what pizza should be, and what toppings make a pizza
how do I know Italians are the right people to ask for what pizza should be?
The inventors should decide what pizza should be
traditional and modern pizzas should co-exist because pizza is not just found in Italy but all over the world
objectivity and subjectivity cannot exist together
Examples of objective and subjective things co-existing
comparing examples doesnt work as hot dogs arent treated on the same level as pizza
The world shouldn’t submit to Italy’s verdict of what pizza should be. But Italy can remain traditional if they don’t cross the line for others

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

arguments.text: ChatGPT is ubiquitous in our daily lives. A recent survey published in the national broadsheet said 4 out of 5 students use ChatGPT (or AI) for homework. There is good reason for this - it helps with summaries, drafting the first cut of essays/arguments, generates ideas etc

Chat Gbt being ubiquitous doesn’t mean it is useful for education. Students need to start learning the fundamentals rather than skip to the answer. A student doing a math problem needs to know the steps that lead to the answer. If the answer is just fed by ChatGBT, the student will not develop the fundamentals properly. Also where is your survey from?

ChatGPT being ubiquitous means its reversal in our lives is nearly impossible, if not very improbable. instead of eradicating it we should learn how to harness its power! It can help explain the steps to answering questions and has endless patience to do so over and over again. it is a teacher’s learning aide and frees them up to do higher value work. the survey of 500 students was conducted for the CNA programme Talking Point - I used chatgpt for this!

That’s true that ChatGBT will continue to be present in our lives and that it can explain the steps to answering questions. However, doing the difficult task of understanding the fundamentals of things and figuring it out for themselves improves a student’s ability to learn and understand, things that will be lost with the use of ChatGBT. Realistically, many students also will not look through the step taught by ChatGBT and skip straight to the answer.

Students know they cannot rely on chatgpt during exams. Although you are right to point out that they are likely to skip straight to the answer when submitting homework, there are other ways teachers can test their understanding during class time. and what about the humanities subjects? students cannot copy the entire answer furnished by AI. furthermore, schools should/already have plagiarism checks to penalise work that is copied off AI.

Yes I do agree that AI cannot be used in exams and teachers can test understanding in class and that plagiarism checks can be used to prevent wholesale copying of answers. However, then why not avoid the use of AI to begin with? Avoiding the use of ChatGBT would promote critical thinking, creativity and deeper understanding of the subject matter.

A ban is not the solution. AI and the things you mentioned (critical thinking, creativity, and deeper understanding) are not incompatible. AI liberates students to become more efficient in their homework and helps them organise their learning in a way that works for them. Some may use it to check if they are on the right track, others use it when they are stuck and cannot move forward. Essentially, it acts as a 24/7 personal tutor for personalized learning.

It is true that AI can improve efficency and organisation. This is useful for a work setting where efficency is important and outcomes are the priority. However, the priority in learning is not efficency, but to develop thinking and intellect. Using AI would hinder students from doing so. Previously when students were stuck, they would be required to think and discuss, rather than find quick-fix answers through AI

reflections.paraphrase: My opponent felt ChatGBT was ubiquitous and useful

It being ubiquitous means negating its use is impossible - and frankly fruitless. Rather than rage against it, we should harness it for exponential learning and growth. ChatGpt doesnt just give you the answer, it teaches you the steps so you fully understand it. And it has limitless patience. As for the survey - you can use ChatGPT to check.
ChatGBT can help to explain the steps of thinking. ChatGBT is a useful teacher’s aide
the main point that chatgpt providing the answer will erode students’ learning of the fundamentals bc they will skip all the way to the answer
My opponent feels that safeguards can be used so that students can still learn well despite the use of AI
Chatgpt erodes understanding so should ban it completely
ChatGBT can improve efficency in doing homework and personalise learning.
AI reduces students’ ability to think

topicQuestion: Go for higher pay at expense of social life (For) or take lower pay to enjoy life (Against)?

arguments.text: My mental health
Higher pay brings long term security from a higher salary. You can use your larger amount of savings to invest into your mental well-being later on.
But if I lose my mental health early then I will have no motivation to carry on earning money for the future
It depends on how much you can earn before you burn out. Also, isn’t it worth it to push your limits? If you believe you can do it, you can get far in life.

It’s true that if I believe I can do it I can go far in life, but sacrificing social life means burnout for me at the expense of my mental health. If I earn less and have a life, I boost my own morale and confidence for the future so that eventually I can slowly earn more progressively. There’s a need to be content with what I have at present so that I have energy to invest in important other things and not swamp myself with work

Keeping mental wellness is one aspect of one’s life. I argue that mental wellness is important, but sacrificing high pay may neglect other areas in life that can be affected by unforeseen circumstances. For example, a health crisis or economic recessions.
But I would have sufficient savings set aside for a rainy day and that boils down to prudent money management. I don’t have to experience the finer things in life to be happy.

Contentment serves you well in this consumeristic culture. My perspective is that if I can take care of my needs, I can take care of others as well so that I don’t become a financial burden. Borrowing money between friends and family can place tensions on the relationships, which I want to avoid.

reflections.paraphrase: lower pay is justifiable if it allows me to keep mental wellness
But if loose my mental health early in life then I will not have motivation to even earn money for the future
mental wellness is key to earning money in a sustainable way
Na
mental wellness helps earn me more money in the long run
prudent financial management will take care of financial emergencies

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

arguments.text: We should not use ChatGPT for homework because it defeats the purpose of learning. ChatGPT gives students a shortcut to reach the solutions for their homework.

ChatGPT can enhance a student’s learning journey instead of inhibiting as it can convey lessons or concepts in a easier to understand manner for the student. Although it does provide shortcuts to their homework, a student can also learn from the solution itself.

Although ChatGPT can help students comprehend the lesson materials more effectively, better than most teachers, it is still important for students to personally and actively engage with the material. Hence, students still need to personally engage with the homework, of their own thinking without the help of ChatGPT. In general, homework questions are also sufficiently clear to not require ChatGPT to comprehend too.

When ChatGPT is used as a tool alongside independent learning - it can help students develop their critical thinking by exposing them to other points of view other than their own. Similarly, it can help develop a student’s existing ideas further. The assumption that homework questions being sufficiently clear to understand by themselves do not consider students who may be weaker at a certain subject or with learning disabilities.

I think it’s a good point that ChatGPT may expose students to alternatives points of view and can help students them to develop their ideas further. However, this is limited to homework that may benefit from disussion. ChatGPT may offer alternative points of view in a convenient manner, however, it also removes group discussion and listening experiences from the student.

I do agree that ChatGPT affect discussion and listening experiences in group settings. However, if the student has no friends in the same class, they can definitely benefit from having an AI companion to discuss projects with. ChatGPT also has a group chat function now - so a group discussion with human friends can be enhanced with AI input.

I think that AI used to supplement a group discussion would be helpful to push the group further. Yet, for students who have no friends in the same class, the onus is on the teacher to assign the student to others in the class. The student need not be paired with people whom they consider "friends".

I agree that teachers can assign study buddies, but the student may still feel awkward around new people. Thus, they would feel more comfortable bouncing off their ideas to ChatGPT as they do not have to hold back their thoughts or opinions in fear of offending the other party.

reflections.paraphrase: My opponent is saying that it can act as a better teacher to the students.
reflections.acknowledgement: No, because it is irrelevant to main point

reflections.paraphrase: ChatGPT should not be used for homework as it removes the learning process for students. It provides students with answers for their homework which is a shortcut.
ChatGPT can inhibit a student’s proactiveness in their own learning and become lazy.
reflections.acknowledgement: Yes, i do agree with the points - some independence is needed for learning

reflections.paraphrase: My opponent is suggesting that ChatGPT can expose students to other points of view and to develop their ideas further. My opponent suggests that the homework questions may not be clear enough for students with learning disabilities.
ChatGPT may remove the need for beneficial group discussions and human interaction
My opponent is suggesting that students with no friends in class will benefit from an AI companion as a supplement. They are also suggesting group discussions can be enhanced with AI
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, I think it is a weak argument to say that the student has no friends in class

reflections.paraphrase: Students do not need to be close friends to benefit from an intellectual discussion
My opponent is saying to use ChatGPT as a social gel between new students.
reflections.acknowledgement: Not really, we are off-topic right now

topicQuestion: Does pineapple belong on pizza?

postDebateSurvey.player1AiAwarenessJustification: I don’t feel like debating with AI
postDebateSurvey.player1DetectionOther: sentence structure + vocabulary used reeks of AI; too clinical and sterile

arguments.text: Pineapple adds a fresh taste to the savoury flavour of pizza
I womited when I ate pineapple on pizza
Clearly you had a traumatic experience, but what caused it? Was it the combination of flavours?
Pineapple == fruit == bad for you
Fruits are part of a healthy diet. You may dislike fruits, but what about an objective view towards this topic?

So the argument is whether pineapple belongs on pizza. Fruit might be necessary for a healthy diet (debatable because I think all you need is energy drinks and feet). But, specifically pineapple tastes terrible on pizza. Therefore, it does not belong on pizza.

You have quite the odd view towards a healthy diet. Dessert pizzas exist, and they bring a sweet flavour as opposed to the usual flavours of traditional pizza. In the case of dessert pizzas, do you think pineapples belong on them?
Nah I don’t pineapples can be even be considered valid food.
Shall we then agree to disagree, since you have very specific tastes?

reflections.paraphrase: My opponent had a bad experience when having pineapple on pizza

topicQuestion: Should we use ChatGPT for homework?

postDebateSurvey.player2DetectionOther: I could see her type beside me.

arguments.text: ChatGPT should not be used for homework as homework trains students to use their minds, and using ChatGPT takes that opportunity away.
using chatgpt helps us have a deeper understanding on the topic we are studying, understanding the topic would lead to us having better contextual knowledge and have better grades
ChatGPT is reliant on findings from the internet which may be untrue, and may misguide the student who relies on it for homework, making them learn something false without them knowing it.
Chatgpt summarises the information found online, lessening our time spent on finding resources and while it may not always be true, we can fact check them using google
While chatgpt may reduce the time spent on homework, nothing beats the satisfaction of deriving answers from scratch, without the help of AI.
Students in schools are only focused on reaching the end point the fastest possible way, the satisfaction comes from completing the work, not the process
ChatGPT steals intellectual properties to accomplish work. It does not teach students to credit sources nor fact check them when giving them seemingly trusted replies quickly.
If more people know to use AI to their advantage while treading carefully around it,

reflections.paraphrase: chatgpt doesn’t train our minds
